summary: Gruber critiques a Slate article by David Auerbach that advised users not to trust Apple with any of their data following the iCloud celebrity photo leak. He argues that this advice is not just hyperbolic but potentially dangerous, because cloud backups are themselves a form of security against data loss. Drawing on stories from Genius Bar staff who dealt with devastated customers who lost irreplaceable photos, he frames the issue as a trade-off between protection from data loss and protection from data theft. He contends that far more people have suffered from irretrievable data loss than from cloud account hijacking, making blanket anti-cloud advice irresponsible.
key_points: Cloud backups are a form of security, not just a vulnerability — they protect against the far more common problem of irretrievable data loss
The proper balance between security and simplicity is inherently a trade-off, and no solution maximizes both simultaneously
Far more people have been traumatized by permanent data loss than by cloud account hijacking
Advising people to distrust all cloud services without acknowledging the risks of local-only storage is itself dangerous advice
The Slate piece relies on the dismissive trope that Apple customers are shallow fools drawn to shiny things
money_quotes: Don't trust Apple "with any of your data" isn't just wrong because it's a hyperbolic overreaction, it's wrong because it's potentially dangerous.
Backups are a form of security — in the same sense that life insurance is a form of security for your children and spouse.
Once it is determined that the photos and videos are irretrievable from the device and have never been backed up, the job of the Genius staffer turns from technician to grief counselor.
Bereavement is not too strong a word.
There's no way around the fact that the proper balance is somewhere in between maximal security and minimal complexity.
The problems here are multifaceted and complicated; "don't trust anything in the cloud" is simplistic and, in its own way, dangerous.

It is thus, in my opinion, terribly irresponsible to advise people to blindly not trust Apple (or Google, or Dropbox, or Microsoft, etc.) with "any of your data" without emphasizing, clearly and adamantly, that by only storing their data on-device, they greatly increase the risk of losing everything.
key_insight: Blanket advice to avoid cloud services after a security breach ignores that cloud backups protect against the far more common and devastating problem of irretrievable data loss.
